_pass: Level 3 Pass 1 + Rounds 1-3 visual polish + worked examples v2 + LA trim + apps + extra exercises + exercises unify v2
title: What interest actually is
deck: Before any formula, four words: principal, rate, time, interest. Once we name them, every calculation in this chapter is a recipe.
objectives: Set up interest problems by identifying principal, rate, and time, and distinguish simple interest from the total accumulated amount.

content.text: Imagine opening two emails on the same morning. The first is a deposit-confirmation from a high-yield savings account: $34 of interest credited last month. The second is the monthly statement from a credit card: $52 of interest owed since the last bill. Both messages use the same word, and the math underneath is exactly the same. The only difference is which direction the money is flowing.

Interest is the dollar amount paid for the temporary use of someone else's money. When we save, the bank rents money from us and pays interest for the privilege. When we borrow, we rent money from a lender and pay interest in the other direction. This section names the four ingredients every interest problem uses, so that the rest of the chapter is a careful application of formulas rather than a guess about which number goes where.
3.1.1  Interest is rent for borrowed money

Every interest situation involves a starting amount of money, a percent that describes the rental fee, and a length of time over which the rental runs. Borrow $1,000 at 5% per year for 2 years, and we will owe $100 of interest on top of the original $1,000. Deposit $1,000 at 5% for 2 years, and the bank pays us the same $100. The two transactions are mirror images: identical math, opposite direction of cash flow.

    <text x="310" y="210" text-anchor="middle" font-family="JetBrains Mono, monospace" font-size="13" fill="#F4F2F8">I = P × r × t  ·  A = P + I</text>
</svg>
content.caption: Figure 3.1. Every interest problem has the same four pieces. Principal times rate times time gives the interest; principal plus interest gives the future value.

content.text: Definition 3.1. The principal P is the starting amount of money. The rate r is the percent (written as a decimal) that the lender charges per unit of time. The time t is the length of the loan or deposit, expressed in the same time units as the rate. The interest I is the dollar amount of rent paid, and the future value (or accumulated amount) A is the principal plus the interest: A = P + I.

Margin noteThe letter A stands for accumulated, the standard symbol in textbooks. Some sources use F for future value; in spreadsheets, the same idea appears as the function FV(). All three notations describe the same quantity: principal plus interest.

The rate and the time must be expressed in the same units, or the answer comes out wrong by a large multiple. A rate of 6% per year with a term of 9 months has to either be converted to a monthly rate (0.5% per month) or have the term converted to a fraction of a year (9/12 = 0.75 years). Either choice works; the trap is forgetting to convert at all and treating “6%” and “9 months” as if they speak the same language.

Common mistakeMultiplying by the percent number directly. A 5% rate is 0.05, not 5. Computing 1,000 × 5 × 2 instead of 1,000 × 0.05 × 2 turns a $100 interest payment into a $10,000 mistake. Always convert the percent to a decimal before it enters any formula.
Name the four pieces first. The formula is just careful multiplication once the pieces are in the right place.

A friend needs a short loan for a car repair. We agree to lend the money at a clearly stated rate and term. The naming step takes ten seconds, the calculation a minute, and the result is the exact dollar amount the friend will hand back at the end of the loan.

Example 3.1. Lending a friend $500 at 6% for two years.

A friend asks to borrow $500 to fix a transmission. We agree on 6% annual simple interest, paid back in full at the end of two years. We want the total amount the friend will owe at that point.

Solution.

Step 1. Name the four pieces. The principal is P = $500. The rate is r = 6% per year. The time is t = 2 years. The unknown is the future value A.

Step 2. Convert the percent to a decimal.

6% = 6 / 100 = 0.06

Step 3. Compute the interest. Multiply principal by decimal rate by time:

I = P × r × t = 500 × 0.06 × 2 = $60

Step 4. Add the interest to the principal.

A = P + I = 500 + 60 = $560

The friend will owe $560 at the end of two years. A quick sanity check: 6% of $500 is $30 of interest per year, and two years gives $60. The total ($560) is a little more than the principal ($500), which is exactly what one would expect of a short loan at a modest rate.

In the wildEvery credit-card statement, mortgage amortization table, auto-loan schedule, savings-account dashboard, bond coupon, and Treasury auction announcement uses the same four quantities, sometimes under different names. “Coupon rate” is just r. “Maturity” is t. “Face value” is P. Naming the pieces once means we can read any of those documents fluently for the rest of the chapter.

content.problem: Deposit $1,000 at 5% annual simple interest for 1 year. How much interest is earned?
A $2,000 deposit earns 4% annual simple interest for 3 years. What is the accumulated amount?
A $1,000 deposit earned $120 in simple interest over 2 years. What annual rate did it pay?
content.answer: 6%. Solving I = Prt for r gives r = I / (Pt) = 120 / (1,000 × 2) = 0.06, or 6%.

content.problem:
In an interest formula, 5% goes in as…

  1. 5
  2. 0.5
  3. 0.05
  4. 5/100, depending on the formula
content.answer: C. The percent symbol divides by 100, so 5% = 0.05.

content.problem:
Nine months, expressed as a value of t in years, is…

  1. 9
  2. 0.9
  3. 0.75
  4. 0.09

If a question asks “how much will the borrower pay back?” it is asking for…

  1. I (the interest only)
  2. P (the principal)
  3. A (principal + interest)
  4. r (the rate)
content.answer: C. “Pay back” means the entire amount due at the end of the term, which is the future value A = P + I.

content.problem: You deposit **$3,000** in a high-yield savings account paying **4.5% per year** (simple interest for this exercise). How much interest do you earn in one year? What is the new balance?
Two friends each lend each other $200, at 3% interest, for 6 months (half a year). How much does each one pay back?

content.title: Interest paid on a savings deposit
content.problem: You deposit **$2,000** in an account that pays **3% per year** in simple interest. After **1 year**, how much interest have you earned, and what is the new balance?
content.solution:
**Interest is the cost of money — and when *you* lend money to the bank by depositing it, the bank pays *you* interest.**

Interest earned: 3% of $2,000 is 0.03 × 2,000 = **$60**.

New balance: $2,000 + $60 = **$2,060**.

Notice the two numbers are different things: the *rate* is **3%** (a percentage), and the *amount* of interest is **$60** (a dollar value). Students often confuse the two.

content.title: Direction matters: borrower vs lender
content.problem: Maria borrows **$500** from her brother and agrees to pay it back in one year with **5% interest**. How much does she pay back? How much did her brother earn?
content.solution:
**The same numbers; opposite signs from each side's view.**

Interest: 5% of $500 = 0.05 × 500 = **$25**.

Maria's payback amount: $500 + $25 = **$525**. (She *paid* $25 in interest as the cost of borrowing.)

Brother's earnings: he gave $500, gets $525 back. **He earned $25** on the loan.

Same $25, different sign depending on which side of the transaction you sit on.

content.title: Summary
content.content: Interest is the price of money over time. When you lend (by depositing, buying a bond, or making a loan), interest is what you earn. When you borrow (mortgage, car loan, credit card), interest is what you pay. The mechanics are identical; only the direction of the cash flow changes.

content.title: Interest in the high-yield-savings era
content.content: From 2010-2020, savings accounts paid essentially zero interest. Banks could get away with it because money market alternatives were also paying nothing. Then, in 2023-2024, Federal Reserve rate hikes pushed online savings accounts (Ally, Marcus, SoFi, Wealthfront) to **4-5% APY** — the highest rates in two decades. Suddenly the math of interest mattered again: a college student with a $3,000 emergency fund at 4.5% APY earns about $135/year — real money for a textbook or two semesters of streaming subscriptions. The lesson is bigger than the dollar amount: keep a fluent ear for what "interest" means and where it's hiding, because rate environments shift, and the math is the constant.

content.text: Section 3.2 picks up the simplest formula in the chapter, I = Prt, and uses it on the kind of short-term lending where interest does not compound: Treasury bills, short-term notes, and most introductory savings problems.
